title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 42-1
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 6.35 per unit for a committed volume, which is in line with the latest reference levels and gives us room to support a reliable supply arrangement. If you’re planning meaningful volume, I’m open to discussing structure and delivery terms to help make this work.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.11.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. I’m seeing a very different workable range on my side, and 6.35 is well above where this program can be approved, even with committed volume. To keep the conversation moving, I can open at 2.05 per unit, and if there’s flexibility on volume planning or delivery structure, we can see whether there’s a practical way to narrow the gap.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.05, below its hidden seller floor of 2.47.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 6.35 per unit for a committed volume, which is in line with the latest reference levels and gives us room to support a reliable supply arrangement. If you’re planning meaningful volume, I’m open to discussing structure and delivery terms to help make this work.

If you’re planning meaningful volume, I’m open to discussing structure and delivery terms to help make this work.', 'from_agent_id': 'supplier'}.

steps.message: I appreciate the response, but 2.05 is not a workable production price for us given current market conditions and the supply commitment you’re asking for. The latest reference point on my side is substantially higher, so to move in good faith I can come down to 5.95 per unit if we’re discussing a real volume program with dependable planning. If you can add forecast visibility or firmer commitment on volume, I may have some room to keep narrowing this from there.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.35 and the added planning language, but that level is still not close to something I can allocate committed capacity against. The current market reference remains materially higher, so I need to reflect both replacement value and the cost of holding volume for you. If you can pair firmer forecast visibility with a steadier call-off profile, I can move to 4.60 per unit as a serious concession from my last position and a practical basis to keep this moving.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 4.60 is still well beyond what I can put into our cost stack, even with improved planning discipline on our side. I can tighten our forecast visibility and work toward a steadier call-off profile, and on that basis I can move to 2.55 per unit as a concrete step from 2.35. If there is flexibility tied to volume commitment or scheduling consistency, that is the range where I can keep this discussion commercially credible.

If you can meet us there with clear allocation and timing terms, we should be close enough to wrap this up next round.

That’s a real effort on our side to close the gap today and recognizes the value of reliable allocation, while still keeping the program workable for us.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
